sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 81, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE still didn't work (board_changed=False). The lower area is still W100 (merged).
I notice something important in the node list when the container was a diamond:
- Node 11: color=g, pixels=43 (this is the diamond outline)
- Node 12: color=W, pixels=85 (this is the diamond interior)
When the container was a rectangle:
- Node 12: color=W, pixels=84 (this is the rectangle)
So the diamond container has TWO components: an outline (g, 43 pixels) and an interior (W, 85 pixels). The rectangle container has just ONE component: the filled rectangle (W, 84 pixels).
Now I see something interesting. When the container is a diamond, there's an additional node (Node 11, g, 43 pixels) that represents the diamond outline. And the interior is W, 85 pixels.
But wait, when I toggled back to rectangle (LEFT), the container became W, 84 pixels again. And SPACE still didn't work.
I'm very confused about why SPACE isn't working. Let me look at the history more carefully to understand when SPACE DID work.
Actually, let me re-examine the SPACE history output. I see entries like:
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=p, pixels=50"  
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=100"
Wait, there are THREE lower area pieces before SPACE: W50, p50, W100. But the reference panel also has W50 and p50. So there are actually FIVE pieces with pixels in [50, 100]:
- Reference white: W50
- Reference purple: p50
- Lower area top: W50 (or p50)
- Lower area bottom: p50 (or W50)
- Lower area merged: W100
When the lower area is SPLIT, there are two pieces: one W50 and one p50.
When the lower area is MERGED, there is one piece: W100.
But in my current state, the lower area is W100 (merged), and SPACE doesn't split it. In the history, SPACE did split it when it was W100. What's different?
Wait, maybe the issue is that in the history, the lower area was NEVER just W100 alone. Let me re-read:
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=p, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=100"
This shows THREE pieces with pixels in [50, 100]. But two of them are the reference panel (W50, p50), and one is the lower area (W100). So when the lower area is merged, there are 3 pieces total with pixels in [50, 100].
And when the lower area is split:
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=p, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=p, pixels=50"
"Lower area before: color=W, pixels=50"
This shows FOUR pieces with pixels in [50, 100]: reference white (W50), reference purple (p50), lower top (p50), lower bottom (W50).
So SPACE toggles between 3 pieces (merged) and 4 pieces (split). But in my current state, there are only 2 pieces with pixels in [50, 100]: reference white (W50), reference purple (p50), and lower area (W100). That's 3 pieces, which should be the "merged" state. And SPACE should split it to 4 pieces.
But SPACE isn't working! Why?
Hmm, wait. Let me count again. In my current state:
- Node 5: W, 50 (reference white)
- Node 9: p, 50 (reference purple)
- Node 13: W, 100 (lower area merged)
That's 3 pieces with pixels in [50, 100]. This matches the "merged" state from the history. So SPACE should split the lower area. But it doesn't.
Wait, maybe I need to check if the reference panel pieces are actually in the same positions as in the history. Maybe the reference panel changed somehow?
Actually, I just realized something. In the diamond container state, I saw:
- Node 5: W, 50 (reference white)
- Node 9: p, 50 (reference purple)
- Node 13: W, 100 (lower area merged)
But I also saw Node 12: W, 85 (diamond interior). And when I toggled back to rectangle, Node 12 became W, 84.
Wait, but in the history, the container was always W, 84 (rectangle) when SPACE worked. What if SPACE only works when the container is a rectangle?
I already tried SPACE with a rectangle container and it didn't work. So that's not it.
Let me try something completely different. What if the issue is that I need to have the lower area in a SPLIT state first, and then SPACE merges it? And the way to split the lower area is NOT through SPACE but through some other action?
What if clicking on the lower area itself splits it? I tried clicking on it earlier (MOUSE row=38, col=31) and it did change the board (gameplay_changed=True), but the nodes didn't change. Let me try clicking on the lower area again and see what happens.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the earlier click result:
"Click lower area: board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True, done=False"
And the nodes after were the same. So clicking the lower area didn't change the segmentation but did change the board. What could have changed?
Maybe clicking the lower area toggles its color? Let me check the ASCII representation of the lower area before and after clicking.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the lower area's ASCII representation
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Lower area (rows 34-43, cols 27-36):")
for i in range(33, 44):
    print(f"  Row {i}: '{ascii_lines[i][26:37]}'")

# Let me also check what the reference panel looks like
print("\nReference panel (rows 3-12, cols 3-12):")
for i in range(2, 13):
    print(f"  Row {i}: '{ascii_lines[i][2:13]}'")

# Let me try clicking on the lower area again and check the ASCII diff
before_ascii = current_frame.ascii
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 31}])
after_ascii = current_frame.ascii

# Find differences
before_lines = before_ascii.split('\n')
after_lines = after_ascii.split('\n')
diff_rows = [i for i in range(len(before_lines)) if before_lines[i] != after_lines[i]]
print(f"\nRows changed after clicking lower area: {diff_rows}")
for r in diff_rows:
    print(f"  Row {r}: '{before_lines[r][26:37]}' -> '{after_lines[r][26:37]}'")

# Check segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nNodes after click:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] in [50, 84, 100]:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
